title: The Art of Shipping: Lessons from Two Years of Solo Building
excerpt: Two years ago I quit my job to build in public. Here's what I wish someone had told me before I shipped my first thing.

content.text: Shipping is not a skill you read about. It's a muscle you tear and rebuild, over and over, until the discomfort of putting something out stops feeling like danger and starts feeling like Tuesday.

Two years ago I quit my job to build in public. I had three side projects gathering dust in private repos, a notes app I'd rewritten four times, and a habit of declaring things "not ready yet" indefinitely. I told myself I was being careful. I was being scared.
The first ship is the hardest

My first public release had eleven users — nine of whom were me testing on different devices. The other two never came back. And yet something changed after that deploy. The work became real. It had a URL. Someone could, in theory, find it.
That feeling — of something existing in the world rather than on your hard drive — is what I'd been avoiding. It's also what makes you better.
Done beats perfect, but done still means done

"Ship fast" is misread as "ship broken." What it actually means is: find the smallest thing that's genuinely complete, and release that. Not a skeleton. Not a stub with a waitlist. Something a person can pick up and use right now, even if it only does one thing.
The two years taught me to ask one question before every release: if this was the only thing I ever shipped, would I be okay with it existing? If yes, it goes out. If no, I cut scope until yes.
The feedback you don't get is the most useful

People who don't bother to tell you something is confusing — they just leave — are your most honest reviewers. Watch your analytics with the same attention you'd give a user sitting next to you. Where do they stop? That's your next fix.
I started keeping a doc called "things nobody said but the numbers showed." It's now longer than any post I've written.
The projects you abandon teach more than the ones you finish

I killed four projects in two years. Each one taught me something the survivors couldn't. A billing integration I abandoned after a week showed me I was building for a customer who didn't exist. A CLI tool I spent three months on — and then quietly let expire — showed me I was more interested in the architecture than the problem.
Knowing when to stop is as learnable as knowing when to ship. Both require you to be honest about what the thing is actually for.
What I'd tell myself on day one

Put it out. Not tomorrow. Before you've written the landing page copy. Before the logo is right. Before you've decided on the pricing. Put it out, watch what happens, and let that be your next task list.
The version you're protecting in your head is not better than the one users will improve by using it. It's just imaginary.
